channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Harm Reduction Policy Built Australia's Tobacco Black Market
item.description: Australia’s tobacco black market shows what happens when policy abandons harm reduction. High cigarette taxes, strict vaping rules and limited legal access to safer nicotine products have helped create space for illicit trade to grow. When smokers are priced out or blocked from lower-risk alternatives, demand does not disappear — it moves into unregulated markets. Better policy should reduce smoking-related harm without handing supply to criminals. (Published on June 22, 2026bywrdnews.org)

item.title: The impact of prohibition on health and ethics | #GFN26 Keynote
item.description: Prohibition raises serious questions for both health and ethics. When safer nicotine products are banned or restricted, people who use nicotine may be pushed towards cigarettes, illicit markets or unsafe supply chains. Harm reduction offers a more ethical approach by recognising real-world behaviour and reducing avoidable risk. Public health policy should protect people from harm, not punish them for failing to meet abstinence-only ideals. (Published on June 23, 2026bygfn.tv)

item.title: Brussels should learn from Sweden instead of trying to sabotage it
item.description: Brussels should be learning from Sweden’s smoke-free progress, not making it harder to sustain. Sweden’s low smoking rates are closely linked to the availability of smoke-free nicotine alternatives, including snus and pouches. EU policy that taxes or restricts these products like cigarettes risks undermining one of Europe’s clearest harm reduction success stories. Regulation should follow real-world outcomes, not sabotage them. (Published on June 23, 2026bysnusforumet.se)

item.title: Don’t Freak Out About Fruit-Flavored Vapes
item.description: Fruit-flavoured vapes are often treated as a youth problem, but for many adult smokers they are part of what makes switching away from cigarettes work. Flavours can help distance people from the taste and ritual of smoking while keeping them within legal, regulated markets. Protecting young people matters, but panic-driven flavour bans risk removing one of the tools adults use to quit combustible tobacco. Regulation should target youth access without undermining harm reduction. (Published on June 23, 2026bythefp.com)

item.title: MAGERO: Who will speak for the smokers struggling to quit?
item.description: Smokers struggling to quit are too often left out of tobacco policy debates. Many have tried conventional methods without success, yet lower-risk alternatives are frequently restricted, stigmatised or treated like cigarettes. Harm reduction starts by recognising smokers as people who need practical options, not moral judgement. Public health should speak for those still facing the greatest risk — and help them move away from combustible tobacco. (Published on June 23, 2026bythe-star.co.ke)
